title: Stay Put vs Move When Lost on Land
summary: If you are truly lost on land but not in immediate danger, staying near your last known location is almost always safer and makes you far easier to find. Move only to escape an active hazard or reach clearly safer ground nearby.
quickAnswer: Stop moving, assess your situation, and stay put unless an immediate hazard forces you to move. Rescuers search from your last known location first.
whenToUse: You realize you are lost and cannot find your way back
You are deciding whether to stay where you are or attempt self-rescue by moving
You are in a group and need to decide whether to send someone for help or stay together
preferredAction: Stay near your last known location, improve your shelter and visibility, and begin signaling for rescue.
backupAction: If an immediate hazard forces movement, move conservatively to the nearest safer ground — slightly higher, to a clearing, or to a trail edge — then stop and begin signaling.
stepByStepActions: Stop moving immediately. Take a breath and assess your situation: injuries, weather, available water, remaining daylight, and any communication options.
Identify any immediate dangers at your current location — rising water, unstable slopes, active fire, lightning exposure, or falling hazards.
If no immediate danger exists, stay near your last known location. Rescuers will begin searching along your planned route and from the last known point.
Improve your shelter and visibility from where you are: find or build wind and rain protection, and make yourself as visible as possible from the air and ground.

If you must move to escape a hazard, choose the most conservative option — slightly higher ground above flood level, a nearby clearing, or a forest edge. Never attempt steep terrain, fast water crossings, or routes through dense vegetation.
Move slowly and deliberately, avoiding steep drop-offs, fast water crossings, avalanche paths, and dense terrain that obscures your location.
Once in a safer spot, stop again. Mark your location if possible and begin signaling for rescue using any available means — whistle, mirror, bright clothing, or fire smoke.
warnings: Wandering far from your last known route can drastically enlarge the search area and delay rescue by hours or days
Self-rescue over difficult terrain without skills or equipment is a leading cause of wilderness injuries and fatalities
whatNotToDo: Do not move just because staying feels passive — movement that enlarges the search area is worse than waiting
Do not attempt to navigate by memory alone in unfamiliar terrain; disorientation increases with fatigue and stress
Do not send the group in multiple directions to search — stay together
Do not abandon a strong shelter or signal position without a clear safety reason
redFlags: Severe weather is imminent and your current location provides no shelter options
Active fire, unstable ground, or rising water threatens your current location
A severely injured person in your group cannot survive without rapid professional medical care — you must weigh the risk of moving vs. calling for help
preparednessTips: Always tell someone your planned route and expected return time before going into the backcountry — this is the single most important factor in fast SAR response
Carry a whistle and a signal mirror or space blanket — they weigh almost nothing and can be seen or heard from a mile away
Download offline maps for your area before departing so you have navigation if cell service is lost

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: NPS guidance on staying near last known location and conserving energy when lost in a national park or wilderness area

sourceReferences.title: Lost Outdoors
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear guidance to stay put once genuinely lost, with limited circumstances where movement is warranted

notes: Consolidated from stay-put-vs-move (canonical, keep slug) and stay-put-vs-move-land (to be archived). The archive guide's v2 already contained a high-quality consolidation of both guides — that content forms the basis of this merged version. Canonical v1 sources (NIST telecom, DOT rail transit) were discarded as inappropriate for wilderness content; archive sources (NPS, American Hiking Society) retained. Archive slug stay-put-vs-move-land should be set to review_status: archived AFTER this merged version is approved and released.

tools.name: Whistle
tools.description: A small device that produces a loud high-pitched sound for signaling and attracting attention
tools.context: Used for signaling rescuers once you have established a safe position

tools.name: Signal Mirror
tools.description: A signal mirror used in survival and emergency preparedness
tools.context: Reflects sunlight to signal rescuers from a distance of a mile or more

tools.name: Emergency blanket
tools.description: A lightweight reflective sheet that retains body heat and provides weather protection
tools.context: Provides shelter and visibility — the reflective surface doubles as a signaling aid

tools.name: Map
tools.description: A printed representation of terrain, trails, and landmarks used for navigation
tools.context: Helps orient your position relative to known landmarks and planned route if available

tools.name: Compass
tools.description: A handheld device that uses magnetic north to determine direction of travel
tools.context: Assists with basic orientation if you must move to escape a hazard

tools.name: Flashlight
tools.description: A battery-powered portable light source for illumination in low-light conditions
tools.context: Provides light for nighttime signaling and shelter improvement

tools.name: Lighter
tools.description: A refillable or disposable device that produces a flame on demand for fire starting
tools.context: Enables fire for warmth, morale, and producing smoke as a rescue signal
